title: Nuno-felted pocket wrap
subtitle: A lightweight nuno-felted wrap with two integral pockets, worked on silk organza
excerpt: Nuno-felt a long shoulder wrap on silk organza with two pockets built into the structure as the wool felts into the silk. The finished wrap is about 160 cm by 50 cm, drapes well, and is warm enough for a cool evening indoors.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional nuno-felting practice. Nuno-felted wraps with integral pockets are an intermediate level project documented in British and Scandinavian felting teaching; the pocket construction uses the standard resist method adapted for nuno work.

glossaryTerms.definition: A felting method in which wool fibres are worked into an open-weave fabric base (commonly silk organza or gauze) using hot soapy water and agitation. The wool migrates through the fabric weave and locks around the threads, bonding fabric and wool into a single textile.
A waterproof barrier placed between layers of a nuno piece to create an opening or a pocket. The resist prevents the two layers from felting together in its footprint.
The agitation phase that causes the wool fibres to contract and pull the silk weave into a crinkled, gathered texture.

content.text: method to create two pocket openings during
: two small pieces of polythene placed beneath the silk on the fold lines prevent those sections from bonding, leaving open pockets. Plan the pocket placement before laying any roving.

items.name: Silk organza or lightweight silk gauze, 180 cm by 60 cm
Merino roving, your choice of colour
Polythene resist pieces, each 20 cm by 20 cm

content.text: Lay the silk on the bamboo mat. Mark lightly with a fabric pen where the two pockets will sit: each pocket starts about 40 cm from the short ends and is 20 cm square. These are the resist positions.
Step 2: Place resists and lay roving

Slide one polythene resist under the silk at each pocket position before laying any roving. The resist sits between the bamboo mat and the silk, preventing the bottom layer of silk from bonding to the top layer at that point. Now lay thin wisps of roving in two layers across the entire silk surface, including over the resist positions.
Step 3: Fold and add the back layer'

Once the front layer is laid, fold the bottom third of the silk up over the roving to create a double layer at the pocket area. The polythene resist now sits between the folded silk layers. Lay roving on the outside of this folded-up section as well.

Pour hot soapy water over the entire surface and press to saturate. Begin rubbing firmly in circular motions across the whole surface for 15 minutes, then roll in the bamboo mat and full by rolling back and forth for 5 minutes per quarter-turn rotation. The silk will gather and shrink around the wool as the fibres migrate through the weave.
Step 5: Remove resists and finish fulling'

Once the fabric has consolidated and the wool is firmly locked in, slide the polythene resists out from the pocket openings. Continue fulling for another 10 minutes to finish the piece. Rinse in cool water, shape flat, and leave to dry on a towel away from direct heat.

items.problem: The pocket openings have felted closed and will not open
items.cause: The resist shifted or was too small to cover the full pocket area during fulling
items.fix: If the opening is only partially closed, carefully tease the silk layers apart with a blunt needle while the piece is still damp. If fully closed, the pocket cannot be recovered without cutting, which will cause fraying.

items.problem: The wrap has felted unevenly with thick areas and thin areas
items.cause: The roving was laid in thick clumps rather than thin, even wisps
items.fix: Even roving coverage is the single most important variable in nuno felting. Pull the roving into thin transparent wisps and lay them evenly; a semi-transparent veil of roving is the right thickness for a lightweight wrap.
